# Literature Review

This chapter surveys the academic foundations relevant to reinforcement learning
for high-frequency trading. It is organized in two parts. The first part reviews
market microstructure — the empirical evidence and theoretical mechanisms that
govern short-horizon price formation in limit order book markets. This
establishes the financial environment in which the agent operates and motivates
the feature engineering choices made in Chapter 4. The second part positions
reinforcement learning as the appropriate methodological framework for this
problem, surveys the key concepts that matter in a trading context, and reviews
prior RL trading applications with attention to action-space design,
transaction-cost modeling, and evaluation rigor. The survey concludes by
identifying the specific gap — the joint alignment of LOB-derived features,
continuous control, and microstructure-aware state design — that this thesis
addresses.
